coredata.dc:title: Lateral size homogeneous and doping degree controllable potassium-doped graphene quantum dots by mechanochemical reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Libin
Xie, Chaoran
Wu, Zixia
Ji, Man
Shan, Jia
Cheng, Pei
Liu, Jianming
Chen, Luya
Zhou, Benqing
Sun, Wenjie
coredata.dc:description: The electron modification effects of alkaline metal on graphene and carbon nanotubes have been widely studied by numerous research groups, but never been explored on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), owing to the limitation of synthetic method. Here, we presented a dopant controllable mechanochemical procedure for potassium-doped GQDs (K-GQDs) preparation: by shifting the synthetic parameters, the doping degree of K can be effectively adjusted with little change of the crystal lateral size. Physiochemical characterizations revealed that the K+ tended to form chemical bonds with carbonyls, pyrrolic and graphic nitrogen-the so-called Lewis acid. Spectral techniques further confirmed the K dopants on the as-mentioned sites could reduce the blue fluorescence that related to the intrinsic state, but enhance the yellow and orange florescence caused by the increase of electron radiative transition rate between π*-nO2p and π*-nN2p simultaneously. Moreover, the K-GQDs displayed benign cytocompatibility and cellular fluorescence imaging performance. Herein, we propose the K+ may play a role in enhancing long wavelength florescence intensity of GQDs, which makes the K-GQDs a promising probe for bioimaging. To our knowledge, this is the first time to report the successful preparation of GQDs chemically doped by K element and reveal the long wavelength florescence enhancement (LWFE) effect of K doping. Our findings suggest that the alkaline metal doping may be an effective technique to overcome the florescence wavelength limitations of GQDs in bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Potassium-doping
Graphene quantum dots
Mechanochemical reaction
Long wavelength florescence enhancement
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) AFM, (b) TEM images, (c) high-resolution TEM images of K-GQDs, (d) Raman spectra, (e) XRD spectra and (f) FTIR spectra of MWCNTs and K-GQDs.
(a) C1s, (b) K2p, (c) N1s and (d) O1s XPS spectra of K-GQDs-1, K-GQDs-2, K-GQDs-3 and K-GQDs-4, respectively.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of K-GQDs (inserted picture: aqueous dispersion of K-GQDs under day light), (b) normalized PL spectra of K-GQDs under 365 nm excitation light (inserted picture: fluorescence of K-GQDs under 365 nm excitation light).
Deconvoluted PL spectra of K-GQDs-1, K-GQDs-2, K-GQDs-3 and K-GQDs-4.
Deconvoluted PL lifetime of K-GQDs at 445 nm, 500 nm, 530 nm, and 560 nm under excitation wavelength of 365 nm.
Fluorescence images of OCM-1 cells incubated with K-GQDs-3 for 6 h, obtained by CLSM under different fluorescence channel (Overlap a: DAPI+458 nm; Overlap b: DAPI+514 nm).
Graphical representation of K-GQDs preparation by the mechanochemical reaction mechanism and the LWFE effect of K dopant.

head.ce:title: Lateral size homogeneous and doping degree controllable potassium-doped graphene quantum dots by mechanochemical reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications, School of Ophthalmology and Optometry, School of Biomedical Engineering, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications, School of Ophthalmology and Optometry, School of Biomedical Engineering, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325035, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210033, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Engineering and Applied Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210033, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, College of Engineering, Shantou University, Shantou 515063, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biomedical Engineering, College of Engineering, Shantou University, Shantou 515063, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electron modification effects of alkaline metal on graphene and carbon nanotubes have been widely studied by numerous research groups, but never been explored on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), owing to the limitation of synthetic method. Here, we presented a dopant controllable mechanochemical procedure for potassium-doped GQDs (K-GQDs) preparation: by shifting the synthetic parameters, the doping degree of K can be effectively adjusted with little change of the crystal lateral size. Physiochemical characterizations revealed that the K+ tended to form chemical bonds with carbonyls, pyrrolic and graphic nitrogen-the so-called Lewis acid. Spectral techniques further confirmed the K dopants on the as-mentioned sites could reduce the blue fluorescence that related to the intrinsic state, but enhance the yellow and orange florescence caused by the increase of electron radiative transition rate between π*-nO2p and π*-nN2p simultaneously. Moreover, the K-GQDs displayed benign cytocompatibility and cellular fluorescence imaging performance. Herein, we propose the K+ may play a role in enhancing long wavelength florescence intensity of GQDs, which makes the K-GQDs a promising probe for bioimaging. To our knowledge, this is the first time to report the successful preparation of GQDs chemically doped by K element and reveal the long wavelength florescence enhancement (LWFE) effect of K doping. Our findings suggest that the alkaline metal doping may be an effective technique to overcome the florescence wavelength limitations of GQDs in bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The phenomenon that carbon materials with small molecular weight (as the impurities) can emit fluorescence under light excitation was first discovered by Xu et al., when they tried to purify the single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) by electrophoretic technology in 2004 [1]. Besides, they also observed the molecular weight dependent fluorescence effect: the fluorescent carbon with smaller molecular weight could emit blue light while the larger one could emit yellow or even orange light. The researchers realized the fluorescent properties of carbon materials were related to their sizes, but the exact mechanism behind the phenomenon was unclear. Until 2008, after the discovery of graphene, the quantum confinement effect caused by size reduction of graphene was reported by Novoselov’s group [2]. According to their observation, graphene dots with small lateral sizes (less than 100 nm), other than zero band gap graphene sheet, existed a band gap owing to the quantum confinement effect (the smaller graphene dots existed broader confinement gap), which could explain the above mentioned phenomenon observed by Xu and co-workers [1]. From then on, advanced preparation techniques were developed systematically [3,4], the photoexcited electronic relaxation mechanism was investigated preliminarily [5], and the unique physiochemical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) began to be studied solely. Apart from the fundamental researches, numerous applied researches were also explored in the past decades [6,7].

Among them, GQDs have shown great potential as fluorescence bioimaging probes owing to their benign optical stability and biocompatibility [8–10]. However, there are still plenty of problems to be solved, one of which is the short wavelength fluorescence emitted by the GQDs (common blue or green fluorescence excited by ultraviolet light [10]). The excitation light with short wavelength can cause photobleaching effect of biological molecular, which may destroy the original structure of cell organelles and influence their function [11]. Besides, the excitation and emission light with short wavelength may suffer from low tissue penetration, which restricts their application in in vivo imaging. To overcome the bottleneck, one possible and simple strategy, as mentioned above, is to increase the lateral sizes of GQDs according to the quantum confinement effect. In fact, the long wavelength fluorescent GQDs have been obtained through this strategy [12,13]. However, as shown in the above works, to achieve the long wavelength fluorescence, the size of GQDs should be increased greatly, which may bring new biological problems. For instance, the endocytosis process might be changed from passive uptake to active transport when the size of the material is larger than 10 nm, and the kidney clearance of materials might be impeded when the size of the material is larger than 5 nm [14–16]. Therefore, it is necessary to find another solution to improve the long wavelength fluorescence without increasing the lateral size of GQDs.

Actually, surface modulation with functional groups or heteroatoms of the graphene lattice has been demonstrated as an effective strategy to tune the band gap of GQDs in the earlier studies [17,18]. The oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N)-containing functional groups or dopants are the most commonly used strategies to modify the skeleton of sp2 carbon through both bottom-up and up-bottom techniques for photoluminescence (PL) tuning [19–22]. Moreover, the PL mechanisms of O and N functionalized GQDs have also been deeply studied [23,24]. Generally, the surface functionalization might cause the red-shift of both the excitation and emission wavelength and induce the excitation-dependent fluorescence of GQDs. Although the exact principle of the electron transition dynamics is still under debate [25–27], it is commonly recognized that the functional group or dopants may introduce intermediate state (n state or so-called surface state) between the intrinsic π and π* state [28–30]. As the band gap between the surface state and the excitation state is narrower than that between the intrinsic state and the excitation state, the electron relaxation from the π* state to the n state often emit longer wavelength photons. Hence, it seems like that the long wavelength fluorescence could be improved by increasing the degree of functionalization simply. However, beside the red-shift effect, the n state also plays a role as surface-trap state, which may enhance the non-radiative relaxation of photo-excited electrons, prolong the fluorescent lifetime, and reduce the fluorescence intensity simultaneously [31,32]. Therefore, the above contradictory phenomenon makes the surface modulation another imperfect solution.

The electron modification effects of alkaline metal on graphene and carbon nanotubes have been widely studied by numerous research groups [33–35]. Moreover, it has been proved that the proper doping degree of alkaline metal was able to enhance the conductivity of few layer graphene [34]. However, the doping effect of alkaline metal has never been explored in GQDs, owing to the limitation of preparation technology. In the present work, we developed a mechanochemical approach for potassium-doped graphene quantum dots (K-GQDs) preparation (Scheme 1 ). By increasing the doping degree of K element, we observed the fluorescent red-shift effect of K-GQDs. According to the physiochemical characterization, the red-shift effect could be ascribed to the K doping on the Lewis acid sites, such as carbonyls, pyrrolic-N and graphic-N. Although the positively charged K (K+) bonded to the specific sites might absorb the π electrons from the sp2 carbon lattice and decrease the intrinsic blue fluorescence, the yellow and orange emission of K-GQDs were enhanced. By combining the results of optical spectrum and the fluorescence lifetime, we suppose that the K atoms might enhance the yellow and orange fluorescence by improving the surface state related photoelectrons transition, which is defined as the long wavelength fluorescence enhancement (LWFE) effect. Moreover, K-GQDs displayed good cytocompatibility and fluorescence imaging performance in vitro. Owing to the long LWFE effect of K doping, the biocompatible K-GQDs with proper doping degrees might be a good candidate for bioimaging application. Although the optical performance of K-GQDs need to be improved further by the future research, we suggest the K doping might be a promising technology to overcome the fluorescence wavelength limitation of GQDs.

Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs, l less than 10 µm, d less than 50 nm) were bought from XFNANO (Nanjing, China). Potassium hydroxide (KOH, electronic grade) was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Rat adrenal pheochromocytoma (PC12) cells were purchased from Wuhan Procell Life Technology (Wuhan, China). Human retinal pigment epithelium (ARPE-19) cells were obtained from the National Collection of Authenticated Cell Cultures (Shanghai, China). Human ocular choroidal melanoma (OCM-1) cells were provided by the Institute of Basic Medical Sciences, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences. DMEM/F12 culture medium and 1640 culture medium were purchased from Gibco (Waltham, MA, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from LONSERA LONSA SCIENCE SRL (Shanghai, China). Gentamicin was purchased from Phygene Life Sciences Company (Fuzhou, China). Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) kit and 4′, 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were purchased from Dojindo (Kumamoto, Japan). Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS) was purchased from Wuhan Procell Life Technology (Wuhan, China).

The K-GQDs were synthesized through the ball-milling method, a kind of typical mechanochemical reaction. MWCNTs as the carbon source and KOH as the K source were selected in this study. To modify the doping level of K solely without influencing the other physiochemical properties of GQDs, we only adjusted the amounts of KOH and kept the other experimental parameters consistent. Typically, 500 mg MWCNTs and different amounts of KOH (100 mg, 500 mg, 2.5 g or 12.5 g) were ball-milled together at a milling rate of 500 rpm for 1000 min. The resultants were washed out by 100 mL deionized water and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for several times. The black precipitates were removed carefully and the translucent supernatant was transferred into the dialysis bags with a molecular weight cut off (MWCO) of 3000 Da for 7 days. The as-prepared GQDs were named as K-GQDs-1, K-GQDs-2, K-GQDs-3 and K-GQDs-4 respectively, according to the different doping degree of K.

In order to obtain a comprehensive understanding of the as-prepared K-GQDs, we characterized the resultants with a variety of technologies. Atomic force microscope (AFM, Multimode 8, Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2001F, Tokyo, Japan) were operated to study the morphological features of K-GQDs. Raman spectrum (Thermo Fischer DXR, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8, Bruker) were selected to explore the graphic crystal structure. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher Scientific) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific) were operated to explore the chemical properties of the K-GQDs.

The optical properties of K-GQDs were characterized by ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (Cary 100, Agilent, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Typically, the aqueous dispersions of different K-GQDs (20 µg/mL) were characterized by UV–Vis adsorption spectrum in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Similarly, to obtain the stable state emission spectra of K-GQDs, the dispersions of different K-GQDs (62.5 µg/mL) were operated in the excitation wavelength range of 200–600 nm with an interval wavelength of 10 nm. In order to get further detailed information, the as-obtained emission spectra were fitted by the Gaussian function with 5 peaks according to the different possible pathways of photoelectronic transition. Subsequently, the excitation spectra were obtained at the longest emission peak (560 nm) by fluorescence spectrophotometer and also fitted by the Gaussian function with the same principle. Finally, the dynamic of photoelectronic transition of K-GQDs was further determined by the time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. As the obtained curves of fluorescent lifetime presented 2 distinct components: the fast component and slow component, the decay curves were fitted by the exponential function (formula 1), where τ1 and τ2 were the fast decay lifetime and slow decay lifetime, and the A1 and A2 were their weighting parameters. The average decay lifetime (τAve) was then calculated by formula 2.

The ARPE-19 cells were cultured in DMEM/F12 medium supplemented with 10% FBS and 0.16% gentamicin solution. The PC12 cells and OCM-1 cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10% FBS and 0.16% gentamicin solution. All cells were incubated in the atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37 °C. The CCK-8 assay was operated to evaluate the viability of APRE-19, PC12 and OCM-1 cell lines after treated with K-GQDs. Specifically, the cells were seeded in 96-well culture plates at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well and pre-incubated (37 °C, 5% CO2) for 24 h. Then the culture medium was replaced with 100 μL of the fresh medium containing different concentration of K-GQDs (25, 50, 100, 150, 200 μg/mL) for incubation of 24 h. To evaluate the cell viability, the medium of each well was replaced with the 100 μL fresh medium containing 10% CCK-8 solution for further incubation of 1 h. Finally, to calculate the survival rate of cells, the absorbance at 450 nm of culture media was measured by a microplate reader (SpectraMax 190, Molecular Devices, San Jose, CA, USA).

OCM-1 cell lines were seeded on slides in 24-well culture plates at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well and pre-incubated (37 °C, 5% CO2) for 24 h. Then the culture medium was replaced with fresh medium containing different concentrations of K-GQDs-3 (50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) and incubated for another 6 h. Then, the cells were washed twice with DPBS to remove extracellular GQDs, fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min and stained with DAPI solution for 5 min. Cellular imaging performance was observed by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, LSM 880 NLO with AiryScan, Zeiss, Jena, German).

Physiochemical properties of K-GQDs were analyzed by different characterization methods. Firstly, the direct morphological characteristics of K-GQDs were obtained by the AFM and TEM technology. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the thickness distributions of 4 kinds of K-GQDs were homogeneous and the thicknesses were similar. The approximate thicknesses of K-GQDs were measured to be 1 ∼ 2 nm, which revealed the typical few layered graphene-like structure of the as-prepared materials. Subsequently, the typical dot-like shape of K-GQDs was observed by the TEM (Fig. 1b and 1c). The lateral size distributions (inserted figures of Fig. 1b) of 4 kinds of K-GQDs ranged from 0.5 ∼ 3 nm, and the average sizes of K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-4 were calculated to be 1.68 nm, 1.63 nm, 1.61 nm and 1.65 nm, respectively. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 1c, the obvious crystal structure could be observed in the high-resolution TEM images of K-GQDs-1, K-GQDs-2 and K-GQDs-3. The lattice spacing of K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-3 was calculated to be 0.22 nm, which was identical with the (100) lattice spacing of graphite crystal. However, with the increase of doping degree, the crystal structure became more and more blurred from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-3. Furthermore, the crystal structure even disappeared in K-GQDs-4. Herein, we hypothesized that the excess doping of K might destroy the graphitic crystal of GQDs.

Besides, the structural properties of K-GQDs were also confirmed by Raman (Fig. 1d) and XRD spectra (Fig. 1e). The ratio of D/G of MWCNTs was calculated to be 0.56, while the counts of D/G were 1.24, 1.25, 1.34, and 1.34 from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-4, respectively. The obvious variation of D/G proportion from MWCNTs to K-GQDs was caused by the increase of edge amounts and defects that induced by the mechanochemical process [36]. In contrast, the slight changes of edges and defects among different K-GQDs leaded to the similar D/G proportion from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-4. Similarly, in the XRD spectra, the characteristic graphite-like (002) peaks localized in ∼ 26° both appeared in MWCNTs and K-GQDs. The peak positions slightly shifted from 26° to 26.5° when the MWCNTs were cut into K-GQD-1, K-GQDs-2 and K-GQDs-3, owing to their different crystalline species [36]. However, the peak position shifted from 26.5° to 26° again when it turned to K-GQDs-4, which indicated the over doping of K might destroy the crystal structure of K-GQDs. Additionally, an obvious peak at about 32° could be observed in K-GQDs-4, which was the characteristic peak of KC24 crystals mainly found in graphite intercalation compounds [37]. For the samples of K-GQDs-1, K-GQDs-2 and K-GQDs-3, the KC24 peaks were not obvious, which can impute to the instability of KC24 crystals in air and the low K doping contents (0.81%-3.74%, Table S1, Supplementary data). However, the K doping content of K-GQDs-4 was much higher (7.82%, Table S1) than that of other samples. Hence, many K atoms were intercalated into the GQDs in K-GQDs-4, and quite a number of KC24 crystals could be preserved in the interlayer and be characterized in the XRD spectrum except for the unstable part. To sum up, the structural properties were obviously changed when MWCNTs were transformed into K-GQDs, while the morphological characteristics and sizes of different K-GQDs were quite uniform. Moreover, when the doping degree of K was too high, the crystal structure of K-GQDs might be destroyed by the introduction of heteroatoms.

To get a rough chemical information of the K-GQDs, the FTIR was operated firstly. As shown in Fig. 1f, apart from C=C stretching vibration of ∼ 1630 cm−1, adsorption peaks of C=O (∼1750 cm−1) and O–H (∼ 1380 cm−1 and ∼ 3400 cm−1) were also observed, which indicated the O-related functional groups were introduced into the pristine graphic crystals [38]. The quantitative and detailed chemical information was then obtained by XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 a and Fig. S1, similar to the FTIR results, obvious O1s peaks were only observed in K-GQDs but not in pristine MWCNTs. The C-N peaks at ∼ 285.6, C-O peaks at ∼ 286.4 and C=O peaks at 288.4 in high resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 2a) further confirmed the existing of O and N elements [38]. Because just the solid MWCNTs and KOH were selected as the reactants and dopants in this study, we hypothesized the air with mixture gases (O2, N2, and CO2 et al.) were also acted as the dopants which formed the C=O and C-N bonds in K-GQDs. In fact, the air has been proved to be doped into the graphene edges during the ball-milling procedure, and the specific gas (N2 or O2) has also been selected as an effective dopant for graphene modification in the mechanochemical process [39].

Interestingly, the peak intensities of K2s, K2p, K3p and K3s increased from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-4 (Fig. 2b and Fig. S2), but the peak intensities of N1s changed inversely (Fig. 2c). The peak intensities of K2p1/2 in the high-resolution K2p spectra (Fig. 2b) also exhibited the same tendency. As the K2s, K3s, and K3p can be ascribed to the K-N bond, we can make a conclusion that the K content doping into the N sites could be increased along with the increase of KOH amounts [40,41]. Besides, the K2p peaks binding energy of K-GQDs was 293 eV, which was lower than the K2p binding energy of physically adsorbed K (∼ 294 eV), indicated that the K formed chemical bond with C [42]. However, it is worthy to note that the pristine K-C chemical bond was also unstable in air. Herein, it was very likely that the K formed chemical bond with the electronegative sites of GQDs. Furthermore, as the K2p1/2 (295.4 eV) and K2p3/2 (292.6 eV) binding energy of K-GQDs were higher than the chemical adsorbed K in the vacancy sites of graphene [41], we can make a careful conclusion that the K+ tended to form chemical bonds at the Lewis acid sites such as carbonyls, pyrrolic and graphic nitrogen. Additionally, according to the N1s spectra, the decreasing of N percentage was mainly caused by the reduction of amide-N (∼ 401.8), while the graphic-N (400.5) and pyrrolic-N (399.6) merely changed [38,43,44]. The reason of this phenomenon might be that the C=O sites were occupied partially by K atoms, which made them unable to bind amide-N, and thus induced the reduction of amide-N. Therefore, we hypothesized that the positively charged K ions were mainly doped into the C=O, pyrrolic-N or graphic-N sites-the so-called Lewis acid.

Unlike the progressive increasing and decreasing tendencies of K and amide-N elements, the variation of O was much complicated (Fig. 2d). Although the proportion of C-O/C=O increased from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-2 obviously, it decreased from K-GQDs-2 to K-GQDs-4 gradually. We can carefully conclude that at higher MWCNTs/KOH ratio (from 5:1 to 1:1), more –OH groups from KOH were grafted into the graphic crystal, but the doping degree of K was merely enhanced. However, when the MWCNTs/KOH ratio was low (from 1:1 to 1:25), the –OH groups grafted into the graphic crystal would decrease with the increase of KOH amount, and the K doping degree was obviously increased simultaneously. It is very likely that the K doping into GQDs may also affect the grafting of –OH by occupying the active C site. For further comprehensive and intuitive comparison, the quantitative variation of overall elements based on the XPS results was listed in Table S1.

To explore the optical properties of K-GQDs, the UV–Vis adsorption spectra were operated firstly. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the absorption in visible light area increased from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-4, which was consist with the phenomenon that the colors of dispersions with the same concentration were different from 4 kinds of K-GQDs. Besides, there were 3 major absorption peaks in all kinds of K-GQDs: one strong absorption peak below 250 nm (π-π*), one shoulder peak near 300 nm (nO2p-π*) and one weak peak near 450 nm (nN2p-π*) [45]. Although the absorption areas in visible light area were various among K-GQDs, the same peak positions strongly suggested that the fluorophores of the different K-GQDs were identical. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence of K-GQDs excited by 365 nm ultraviolet light also shifted from blue to orange with the same tendency, which was then further confirmed by the PL spectra.

To explicate the relationship between chemical composition and fluorescence color, PL spectra were operated in a wide excitation wavelength range from 200 to 600 nm. The excitation-dependent multicolor PL properties of K-GQDs were also observed, similar to previous report [45]. Besides, with the increase of excitation wavelength, we observed 5 emission peaks in the K-GQDs: ∼ 400 nm, ∼ 445 nm, ∼ 495 nm, ∼ 530 nm and ∼ 560 nm (except for the second harmonics peaks). To explore all possible excitation states of K-GQDs, the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were also obtained at the maximum emission wavelength (560 nm) and deconvoluted by the Gaussian fitting. As shown in Fig. S3, the excitation spectra of various K-GQDs could be divided into 5 peaks according to the possible photoelectrons transition states (nN2p-σ*, π-π*, nO2p(C-OH)-π*, nO2p(C=O)-π* and nN2p-π*, respectively) [45,46]. Interestingly, the excitation peak positions of the 4 kinds of K-GQDs were quite similar, which further confirmed that their fluorophores derived from the photoelectrons transition among different states were identical. Compared with the as-reported N-doped oxidized GQDs, there was no novel electronic state observed in the present work [45,46]. In other words, the K dopant did not introduce any mid-state into the GQDs. However, the PL intensity was greatly affected by the K doping.

To distinguish the K doping effect on 5 different fluorophores, the PL spectra were divided into 5 stages according to the deconvoluted PLE spectra. As mentioned above, the first stage was induced by the photoelectrons transition between nN2p-σ*. In this stage, the strongest emission peaks of 4 kinds of K-GQDs were presented in 445 nm, although the fluorescent intensities were decreased gradually from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-4, along with broadened PL band simultaneously (Fig. S4). It was worth to note that the optimum excitation wavelength of K-GQDs-1 was 260 nm, while the optimum excitation wavelength of K-GQDs-2 to K-GQDs-4 was 270 nm. Moreover, the same tendency and phenomenon were also observed in the stage 2 induced by the direct photoelectrons transition between π-π* (Fig. S5), although the PL intensities at 445 nm of stage 2 were much stronger than stage 1. As mentioned above, the morphological characteristics and lateral sizes of K-GQDs were little changed by varying reaction parameters, which has been observed by TEM and AFM. It seems like that the slight red-shift of optimum excitation wavelength between K-GQDs-1 and the other K-GQDs might be caused by the heteroatoms doping-induced edge structural alterations [47]. However, by combining the UV–Vis spectra and the full-wavelength PL spectra, we can make a conclusion that the edge structural alterations had little influence on the surface-state related optical properties of K-GQDs.

When it turned to stage 3 dominated by nO2p(C-OH)-π* electrons transition, the rapid fluorescence decay at 445 nm was observed in all kinds of K-GQDs, accompanied with the red-shift of strongest PL emission peak (Fig. S6). For K-GQDs-1 and K-GQDs-2, the strongest PL peaks changed from 445 nm to 495 nm. However, the strongest PL peaks shifted from 445 nm to 530 nm for K-GQDs-3 and K-GQDs-4, while the shoulder peaks at 495 nm could also be observed. Besides, the PL intensity of K-GQDs-1 decayed rapidly with the increase of excitation wavelength. Unlike K-GQDs-1, the rapid decay tendency of K-GQDs-2 slowed down from excitation wavelength of 370 nm, which might be caused by the fluorescence emission increasing at 495 nm. Interestingly, the PL decay tendencies of K-GQDs-3 and K-GQDs-4 were much slower owing to the longer wavelength fluorescence emission. The fluorescence decay tendency of K-GQDs-1 continued in stage 4 (Fig. S7), owing to the poor long wavelength emission. In contrast, the decay tendencies of K-GQDs-2 to K-GQDs-4 were reversed with the rising of long wavelength emission. Interestingly, the K-GQDs-3 presented the best fluorescence performance in this stage. In fact, the K-GQDs-3 also exhibited the strongest PL performance in stage 5 (Fig. S8), although the intensity decayed with the increase of excitation wavelength. To sum up, it is likely that the K-GQDs-1 with a relatively low K doping degree present a typical red-edge effect: the fluorescent emission wavelength red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelength, accompanied by the decreasing of PL intensity [27]. However, this effect seems to be inhibited by the increasing of K doping degree.

To explore the influence of K doping in each specific electron transitional pathways, the PL spectra excited at the optimum excitation wavelength with 5 peaks were also fitted by Gaussian function. As shown in Fig. 4 , the PL peak of 400 nm (purple area) induced by σ*-nN2p and the PL peak of 445 nm (blue area) induced by π*-π both decreased from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-4. However, the mechanism behind the phenomenon might be quite different. The decreasing of purple area might be induced by the reduction of N dopants, which leaded to the reduction of photoexcited nN2p electrons. On the other hand, the decreasing of blue area might be caused by the reduction π electrons, which were attracted to the Lewis acidic sites induced by the K. In other words, it seems like that the increase of K doping did not influence the σ*-nN2p electrons transitional pathway, but reduced the π*-π electrons transition.

When it turned to stage 3, the PL intensity of K-GQDs shifted with the variation of –OH content calculated by XPS. Similar with the σ*-nN2p pathway, it seems like that the π*-nO2p(C-OH) pathway was also unaffected by the K doping. Interestingly, the PL intensity of 530 nm and 560 nm increased from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-3, but decreased in K-GQDs-4. It is noteworthy that the C=O content decreased from K-GQD-1 to K-GQDs-2, and increased from K-GQDs-2 to K-GQDs-4 oppositely (Table S1). Besides, the N doping degree decreased from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-4. The inconsistent of C=O and N contents with their related fluorescence of K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-3 strongly revealed the LWFE effect of K doping, while the decreasing PL intensity of K-GQDs-4 revealed that the K induced LWFE effect might only take effect in a proper doping degree as the over doping of heteroatoms might destroy the crystal structure of GQDs.

To take a deep insight into the mechanism of LWFE effect, the time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technology was carried out to explore the dynamic of photoelectron transition. As shown in Fig. S9, there were two periods in the decay curves of various K-GQDs. At the early period, the PL lifetime of K-GQDs prolonged with the red-shift of emission wavelength. This phenomenon has been widely reported and ascribed to the enhancement of non-radiative relaxation process [24]. On the contrary, at the late period, the PL lifetime of K-GQDs decreased with the red-shift of emission wavelength. This contradictory phenomenon revealed that there were at least two possible decay components existing in K-GQDs. Herein, we deconvoluted the decay curves into 2 decay components by formula 1: the fast decay component (less than 3 ns) and the slow component (6 ∼ 10 ns). As shown in Fig. 5 , the average lifetimes of different emission wavelength decreased from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-3, while slightly changed from K-GQDs-3 to K-GQDs-4. According to the calculated results, the τAve decreasing of from K-GQDs-1 to K-GQDs-3 at 445 nm and 500 nm was mainly caused by the reduction of either τ1 or τ2. However, the lifetime decreasing at 530 nm and 560 nm was also accompanied with the proportion increasing of A2/A1, which indicated the increasing of slow decay component.

According to previous studies, the long PL lifetime was related to the non-radiative relaxation process [27]. The reduction of PL lifetime caused by the K doping might increase the radiative relaxation of photoexcited electrons and thus enhance the full-wavelength fluorescence performance [23]. However, this present study demonstrated the fluorescent enhancement effect only induced by the decreasing of PL lifetime was very limited. By combining the PL lifetime results with the stable state PL spectra, we can conclude that it is the increase of slow decay component proportion leading to the enhancement of the long wavelength PL performance. Since the slow decay component is ascribed to the surface state related electrons transition, we hypothesize that the mentioned LWFE effect is mainly induced by the increase of photoelectron transition from the π* state to the nO2p(C=O) and the nN2p state [48].

The cytocompatibility of K-GQDs were determined in the cancer cell line of OCM-1 and the normal cell lines of APRE-19 and PC12 by the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. S10, after 24 h incubation, all cell lines remained a higher viability than 85%, even at concentrations up to 200 μg/mL, which indicated their desirable biocompatibility for further applications. Inspired by the good long wavelength fluorescent properties, K-GQDs were expected to be promising for bioimaging applications. Owing to their best fluorescence performance in long wavelength region, biocompatible K-GQDs-3 were selected for the cellular imaging study. After co-incubating with OCM-1 cells for 6 h, the bioimaging performance of K-GQDs-3 in tumor cell was evaluated by CLSM intuitively.

As shown in Fig. 6 , compared with the control group, the cells co-incubated with K-GQDs-3 emitted yellow and orange fluorescence under excitation wavelength of 458 nm and 514 nm respectively, which was consistent with the stable state PL characteristics of K-GQDs-3. In order to study the distribution of K-GQDs-3 in cells, DAPI, a kind of nuclear targeting dye, was selected to label the nuclear of OCM-1 cells. By merging fluorescence images under different channels, we found that the fluorescence intensity of cell nucleus was much higher than that of cytoplasm, which was much more obvious in higher concentration of K-GQDs-3. Due to their ultra-small sizes, it was very likely that the K-GQDs-3 were internalized into the cells by passive diffusion and then entered the nucleus by bypassing lysosomal pathway [49]. As summarized by Wei group [50], although numerous articles have reported the fluorescence enhancement effect of heteroatoms doping (such as N, S, and B et al.), the long wavelength emission of small sized GQDs has merely been achieved. The long wavelength fluorescent GQDs were often large sized, which further restricted their entering into the nuclear of cells [10]. In contrast, the K-GQDs prepared in this study could emit long wavelength fluorescence (yellow and orange emission) rather than the common blue or green emission [10,50], and could effectively enter into the nuclear of OCM-1 cells owing to their very small lateral sizes. This work provides an effective strategy to solve the above contradiction between size and fluorescent wavelength of GQDs in cellular imaging applications. Therefore, the displayed long wavelength fluorescence, good biocompatibility, as well as the benign cell penetrability of K-GQDs-3 make them promising fluorescent probes for cellular imaging and even excellent carriers for nuclear-targeted drug delivery.

In this work, a novel mechanochemical technology was successfully developed to synthesis potassium doped graphene quantum dots (K-GQDs) for the first time. The lateral size of the as-prepared K-GQDs was uniform and the K doping degree was controllable, which enable us to explore the doping effects of this alkali metal further. By operating comprehensive physiochemical characterization and optical technologies, we discovered the long wavelength fluorescence enhancement (LWFE) effect of K dopants. Besides, the benign cytocompatibility and good cell imaging performance of K-GQDs were also confirmed in the present work. This study suggests that K doping with a proper degree might be a possible solution to overcome the fluorescence wavelength limitation of GQDs in bioimaging applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number LQ21H120008), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (grant number 2020M671680), the funding of School of Optometry and Ophthalmology and Eye Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University (KYQD20200402), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61805161).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.135800.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Roles of nitrogen functionalities in enhancing the excitation-independent green-color photoluminescence of graphene oxide dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.Y. Teng, B.S. Nguyen, T.F. Yeh, Y.L. Lee, S.J. Chen, H. Teng, Roles of nitrogen functionalities in enhancing the excitation-independent green-color photoluminescence of graphene oxide dots, Nanoscale 9(24) (2017) 8256-8265. https://doi.org/10.1039/c7nr01037k.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The influence of edge structure on the electronic properties of graphene quantum dots and nanoribbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.A. Ritter, J.W. Lyding, The influence of edge structure on the electronic properties of graphene quantum dots and nanoribbons, Nat. Mater. 8(3) (2009) 235-242. https://doi.org/10.1038/nmat2378.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.B. Tang, R.B. Ji, X.K. Cao, J.Y. Lin, H.X. Jiang, X.M. Li, K.S. Teng, C.M. Luk, S.J. Zeng, J.H. Hao, S.P. Lau, Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots, ACS Nano 6(6) (2012) 5102-5110. https://doi.org/10.1021/nn300760g.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: How successful is nuclear targeting by nanocarriers?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.N. Tammam, H.M.E. Azzazy, A. Lamprecht, How successful is nuclear targeting by nanocarriers?, J. Controlled Release 229 (2016) 140-153. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jconrel.2016.03.022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots for optical bioimaging
